title: Slate’s Gray $25,000 Truck Just Got a Crayola Makeover
gist: Bezos-backed Slate Auto's $24,950 EV truck—the cheapest new truck in the US—is built around a deliberately un-painted, wrappable gray body and an accessories marketplace, and a new $1,550 Crayola wrap partnership (3x the standard wrap price) tests the limits of its 'affordable customization' pitch; the low price is underpinned by a cheaper US-invented, China-perfected LFP battery.

insights.claim: Slate's basic 'blank' truck starts at $24,950—the cheapest new truck on the US market—shipping with a single gray composite body, no touchscreen, and manual windows, all to hit that price point.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A sub-$25K new EV truck is a genuine price disruptor in the US market; watch whether the stripped-spec approach converts to volume orders.
insights.quote: The basic "blank" Slate truck starts at $24,950, a price that has made it the cheapest new truck on the US market.

insights.claim: The truck is designed from the start to be wrapped rather than painted, turning customization into an accessories business: 100+ hue wrap kits from $500 plus more than 200 other accessories sold through Slate's online marketplace.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Slate's real margin play may be a high-attach accessories/marketplace model layered on a razor-thin-margin base vehicle—the recurring-revenue angle to watch.
insights.quote: a gray, unpainted truck designed from the start to be wrapped rather than sprayed to keep costs down

insights.claim: The Crayola wrap starter packs cost $1,550—three times the price of a standard Slate wrap—a meaningful jump that sits in tension with the brand's 'affordable customization' pitch.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Premium-priced branded accessories reveal how Slate intends to upsell margin beyond the loss-leader base truck—test of pricing power on a value brand.
insights.quote: A Dandelion or Jersey Tomato transformation will set owners back $1,550—three times the cost of a standard Slate wrap—a meaningful jump considering the brand's "affordable customization" pitch.
insights.locator: Wraps for the Slate have been part

insights.claim: The truck's low price is enabled by a 65-kWh lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery—a chemistry invented in the US but perfected in China—that is cheaper than traditional nickel-manganese-cobalt packs.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: LFP's cost advantage underpins the sub-$25K price, but 'perfected in China' flags supply-chain and tariff exposure for a US-market budget EV.
insights.quote: It's a lithium iron phosphate battery pack, a technology invented in the US but perfected in China, that's cheaper than traditional nickel-manganese-cobalt batteries.
insights.locator: The Slate's 65-kWh battery pack also explains

insights.claim: Despite the cost-cutting the truck charges at DC 120 kW, going from 20 to 80 percent in about 30 minutes, alongside a claimed 205-mile range, 2,000-lb tow rating, 1,550-lb payload, and 8-second 0-60.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Adequate charging and range specs at the price suggest the vehicle isn't spec-crippled where it matters most—supporting the value proposition against pricier rivals.
insights.quote: the Slate can charge at DC 120 kW, meaning it can go from 20 to 80 percent in just 30 minutes
insights.locator: The Slate's 65-kWh battery pack also explains

insights.claim: Deliveries are stated to begin in Q4 2026 with most units shipping through 2027, and buyers can preorder for $300 (or $250 for existing reservation holders).
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A 2026-2027 delivery timeline and low deposit set the execution milestones to track for whether Slate can convert hype into shipped volume.
insights.quote: deliveries, which the company states are still on track to begin in the fourth quarter of 2026, though most are expected to ship through 2027
insights.locator: As we reported at the end of June
